Friend (petite rectangle, under 159): rectangle at best body-wise but NOT petite_friendly. Strong scale miss.

This is the kind of item that doesn't serve any of the four stage 1 test users — flagged clearly. It anchors cool_tlv for unidentified users; behavior data at scale will validate or invalidate the world's market viability.

product_name: The '90s Crewneck Tee in Compact Cotton
target_user_description: The triangle woman, petite through mid-tall, living in cool_casual, who wants a slim body-following tee with a low-key contrast ringer detail. The ringer carries a quiet sporty register — vintage athletic reference, not statement — earning cool_tlv adjacency. Close fit traces upper body; waist-length hem terminates above hip (triangle-friendly geometry). Rectangle and hourglass workable in ok register (rectangle: traced but no active waist creation; hourglass: lean variation only — no stretch + close cap sleeve strains for fuller bust). Risky for inverted_triangle (cap sleeve + close fit emphasizes shoulders) and oval (close fit at midsection traces contour). First petite_friendly item in catalog
why_selected: First genuinely petite_friendly item in the catalog — directly addresses the most urgent launch gap (Lora, who needs petite + low-exposure). Adds Madewell as a new brand. Also brings a non-neutral colorway (navy body with an electric-blue contrast ringer) into a catalog that skews neutral, and demonstrates the "basic with an earned adjacent" pattern: the plain version of this tee would carry no style tag, a graphic version would read urban_ease, and this colorway's low-key ringer earns a single cool_casual adjacent without committing a core.
known_tradeoffs: Risky for oval (close fit + medium visibility at the midsection; override only in a celebrate/trace-permitted profile, not default). Demoted hourglass and inverted_triangle to "ok," not "best": 100% cotton with no elastane plus a close cap sleeve assumes a lean upper body, so a fuller bust or fleshier arm strains the fit — this is the lean-hourglass item, not the full-bust one. No has_stretch (no elastane), so less forgiving than a jersey tee. Cap sleeve is the opposite of full_arm_friendly. Single garment, not convertible (closed).
tagging_notes:
trace only (no styling_anchor, no neutral): the slim compact-cotton body traces a lean torso per Principle 4, and the bellybutton/high-hip length is too long to read as a cropped styling_anchor and too slim/short to give a meaningful tuck — so unlike item 4's true waist-length polo, it doesn't earn the anchor. silhouette = top_standard, not top_fitted_bodice: the shape comes from the slim cut, not construction (no princess seams/darting), so "fitted" lives in closeness_to_body_top = close. sleeves = cap (raglan cap on the navy version). color_density = has_some_color for the contrast ringer against navy; pattern stays solid (no print). body_visibility_demand = medium, not high: compact cotton is dense and holds shape — shows body shape without clinging — which also means fabric_risks = none (compact cotton resists the cling a thin jersey would have). fabric_quality = acceptable: Madewell is solid mid-tier and the U.S. Cotton Trust Protocol is a sourcing signal, not premium construction, so it doesn't reach the MD/Everlane "high" bar. body_type_best = rectangle, triangle (clean torso line on a straight figure; nothing happening below the waist leaves a pear's lower body alone), ok = hourglass, inverted_triangle, risky = oval. short_torso_friendly applies cleanly (close fit + waist length). Style: item_role = basic, core_style = none, adjacent_style = cool_casual — the ringer is a whisper, not a statement, so it earns a soft adjacent (the schema's "basics CAN carry adjacent_style") rather than a core (Principle 9 — don't commit a world to a single detail) or bridge (only one world is touched). Test-user fit: scale finally serves Lora — petite_friendly is a catalog first — though with the honest caveat that a slim body-tracing tee with a contrast ringer is more body-aware and slightly more sporty than her very-closed/easy register, so it's a scale win and a useful diagnostic rather than a confident style match. Serves Mom cleanly (lean hourglass, mid-height, trace-friendly). Sister is an "ok," not a "best": her fuller bust/arm plus no-stretch cotton is exactly what this item doesn't serve. Misses You mostly on register (you skew more open/directional). Net: real value as the catalog's first petite anchor.
Style world: item_role basic with cool_casual + cool_tlv adjacent. The contrast ringer is a quiet sporty register — vintage athletic reference rather than statement design. Earns dual adjacency: cool_casual (slim basic tee with quiet color accent) AND cool_tlv (classic cut with subtle sporty/anti-trend register). The ringer is too quiet to anchor either world as core; it's a basic with two adjacent registers.
Framework principle locked: items with classic cuts carrying a quiet sporty register (vintage athletic reference, ringer trims, raglan construction, subtle athletic detailing) earn cool_tlv adjacency. The cool_tlv world includes self-conscious-sporty as part of its anti-trend vocabulary, distinct from active_leisure functional sportswear. Cool_tlv core requires stronger refusal-of-flattery or directional commitment; adjacent welcomes the quiet sporty accen

intent_execution_notes:
intent_2: "Worn with a top tucked into the structured high waistband (flat front, belt loops). The waistband is a clean anchor surface, so the tuck makes the meeting point the waist she defines. styling_anchor strategy. Left untucked the same short steps back (Intent 6) — tucking is her choice, and the high rise is what lets her make the waist. Fires for the independent definer (Q5a = tuck)."


intent_6: "Worn with a relaxed or oversized top left out over the high-rise short. Soft linen drape + relaxed cut keep the short body-disengaged — nothing tucked, cinched, or featured; the whole look steps back from the body. The bare leg below the mid-thigh hem reads easy and incidental, not a staged opening. neutral strategy. Fires for the step-back register (Q5a = leave, no-build, relaxed top + relaxed bottom)."
target_user_description: The rectangle or hourglass woman, petite through tall, living in cool_casual with bridge-flexibility into refined_casual, who wants a tailored high-rise linen short that adapts across registers. Plays styling_anchor (tucked fitted top creates waist event via high-rise) and neutral (untucked relaxed top covers waistband, short fades into background). First confident petite_friendly + cool_casual + low-exposure piece in catalog. Triangle risky default — 3" inseam hits her widest zone. Override fires when she accepts short hems + celebrates legs + commits to Q6 = top and bottom same. Inverted_triangle and oval workable in ok register.
why_selected: Second confirmed petite_friendly item in the catalog — directly serves Lora's most urgent gap, and unlike item 27 (the ringer tee, which is petite on scale but borderline on register), this one is a confident style match: plain black, more covered through the design intent, low body visibility. Petite confirmation comes from a self-identified "extra petite" review explicitly naming the structural reasons (clean leg fall, back elastic, 100% natural fabric). Also: first true bridge item between cool_casual and refined_casual via genuine dual-strategy capability (styling_anchor + neutral) rather than just marketing claims. Reinforces Madewell as a brand, adds high-quality 100% linen to the catalog, and fills the non-denim summer bottoms gap flagged in Part 8.
known_tradeoffs:
Triangle risky default — 3" inseam at her widest zone (mid-thigh). Override fires when she accepts short length on skirts/dresses (universal short-hem signal) AND celebrates legs in featuring zones AND commits to Q6 = top and bottom same (balanced proportion). Without all three, triangle is filtered.

Oval at 'ok' — high-rise at natural waist (concern zone) softened by back elastic + linen drape forgiving behavior. Inverted_triangle at 'ok' — short hem adds lower-body presence (positive) but high-rise doesn't specifically serve her shoulder-balance need.

Wrinkles easily (100% linen). No has_stretch (back elastic is waistband only, not woven). 3" inseam reads proportionally short on tall (175+) — soft cap at tall_friendly. fabric_quality: high (premium natural linen, tailored construction).
tagging_notes: Dual strategy = styling_anchor + neutral, validated by the brown colorway model styling: the 12⅛" rise commits to a high-waist anchor when tucked, but a hip-length untucked top covers the waistband cleanly and the short fades into background — textbook neutral per the schema. Single garment with two real modes, like the MD button-downs but executed differently. fabric_behavior = drape rather than structured: 100% linen has body but isn't rigid like Cone denim or ponte; it falls cleanly when cut tailored, breaks and creases under hand, doesn't impose silhouette. closeness_to_body_top = relaxed (not blank): captures how the short reads when paired with an untucked relaxed top — the outfit sits relaxed, even though the short itself has no top. volume = none: clean leg fall per the petite reviewer's explicit "they do not balloon wide in the legs," fails the "remove closeness, does volume mean anything?" test. fabric_quality = high: 100% linen premium natural material, genuine tailored construction (slanted front pockets, back patch pockets, hidden back elastic, real button/zip front), Madewell mid-brand with consistent execution (4.8/18 reviews) — same tier as MD linen polos, softer-high than Everlane. body_type_best = rectangle, hourglass, triangle (clean tailored line works on straight, defines via high rise on balanced, doesn't add lower-body bulk on pear); ok = inverted_triangle (adds some lower-body interest, fine but not optimal) and oval (high rise above midsection + back elastic + drape are all forgiving — moved up from risky on second look). No risky tag — rare but earned: forgiving fabric + back elastic + high rise + clean leg = pretty universally compatible. body_modulators: midsection_friendly (back elastic explicitly designed for "puffy days" per the reviewer) and curvy_lower_body_friendly (clean leg fall doesn't add width to existing curves — the cut is friendly precisely because it doesn't balloon). fit_failures_addressed = forgiving_midsection only — has_stretch threshold not met (back-only elastic, not woven stretch). item_role = bridge across cool_casual core: the dual-strategy capability makes the office-to-vacation positioning real, not just marketing — it genuinely adapts across registers, which is the bridge definition (connects across worlds), not hero (commits and defines one). Test-user fit: strong match for Lora — petite_friendly confirmed, plain black, low body visibility, more covered/quiet than item 27. Mom and Sister both well-served (hourglass best, mid-height scale, refined_casual access via tucked styling). Misses You least on fit than on personal register (you skew more directional). Net: the catalog's first confident petite + cool_casual + low-exposure piece, plus the first genuine cross-register bridge in shorts.

intent_execution_notes:
intent_2: "Front-tucked or half-tucked into a high-rise; the loose body blouses over the waistband so the meeting point becomes a soft, self-defined waist she controls — full tuck for a cleaner line, front-tuck for ease. styling_anchor strategy: the shirt is the affordance, she is the agent. Worn buttoned-and-loose it's Intent 6 — the waist appears only on her terms, which is exactly the independent-definer move (Q5a = tuck, no-build)."

intent_6: "Worn untucked or open as a layer over a tee/bra. The loose lyocell drape falls past the waist; body disengaged through torso. Pure step-back composition in the refined_casual register. neutral strategy. The premium fabric and considered cut keep the relaxed mode from reading sloppy."

intent_7: "Full button placket + loose hip-length cut + lyocell drape supports genuine multi-modal capability. The shirt works buttoned closed, partially open (décolletage feature), fully open as a layer (step-back), tucked, or half-tucked. Multi-modal Intent 7 users get one shirt that delivers across their wardrobe modes."

intent_9: "Worn open or partially unbuttoned over a tee or bra. Body covered through torso by loose drape + deliberate design opening at the full button placket reveals décolletage. The V-line from the unbuttoned placket is intentional — full placket construction makes the opening a real visual event, not a small polo notch. Fires for users who named décolletage as featuring zone + prefer cover-and-open-strategically register."
target_user_description: The rectangle, inverted_triangle, or oval woman, mid-short to tall, living in refined_casual with formal_polished access, who wants a quietly elevated white button-down that genuinely covers across registers. Plays two strategies: styling_anchor when tucked or half-tucked into trousers/jeans (creates defined proportion, pushes toward formal_polished), neutral when worn loose untucked or open as an overshirt layer (drape carries a relaxed-elevated weekend look). The dual-fabric story (lyocell drape rather than crisp poplin) is the key — this is the refined-elevated white shirt for a woman who wants softness over crispness, with the option to dress it up by tucking and pairing.
why_selected: Catalog's first true white button-down — a closet staple gap. Adds 100% lyocell as a new fabric (premium natural-derived, drape behavior, low wrinkle risk vs the linen polos), which is genuinely additive in a catalog that's been heavy on cotton and linen. First MD piece since items 1-3 with clear refined_casual core, but distinct from those: full button-down placket (vs half-placket polos) gives genuine exposure flexibility, and drape lyocell softens the body-compatibility range (triangle moves from risky on the linen polos to ok here). Formal_polished adjacent fills a quiet gap — the catalog has had limited access to that register, and this is a soft, achievable entry point. Reinforces MD as the refined_casual anchor brand in the catalog.
known_tradeoffs: Not petite-friendly — 68cm back length sits mid-thigh on a 150cm frame and swamps. Soft drape lyocell is slightly delicate (can snag, holds a relaxed/lived-in hand even new) — reads as elegance for the right user, as under-pressed for someone wanting crisp polish. Hourglass and triangle are "ok," not "best": loose cut needs tucking or half-tucking to define waist on hourglass; on triangle the hip-length over wide hips slightly emphasizes hip width if worn untucked, though drape lyocell is significantly more forgiving than crisp linen (which is why triangle is ok here, not risky as it is on items 1-3). Sleeve length at size 34 (39cm) is on the shorter end — sits at forearm rather than wrist on taller users. Lyocell snag risk worth flagging for users hard on clothes.
tagging_notes: product_type = button_down with silhouette = top_standard, not top_boxy: loose but not square — set-in shoulders, slight body taper, curved shirttail hem per the tech sketch. (Probably worth a quiet audit of items 1-3 against the same call — they may have been over-tagged as boxy.) Dual strategy = styling_anchor + neutral, validated by the full placket: same dual-mode pattern as items 1-3 but executed with more flexibility, since full placket gives true exposure range (vs polos' half-placket structural floor at low). closeness_to_body_top = relaxed (loose cut through body and sleeves per tech sketch and 101cm chest at size 34), volume = none (loose ≠ volume per Principle 3 — no drop shoulders, no sustained box, no exaggerated width; the back box pleat is a construction detail providing movement, not exaggerated volume). edge_break = v_neck: full placket creates a genuine V at chest when worn unbuttoned, which is the intended styling. featuring_zones = decollete (design intent features the zone via open collar + V-line) but exposure_level = none / location = none (per Principle 5: tag at the realistic design minimum, not the styled photo — full placket means she can fully button to the throat; styling_flexibility = open carries the signal that exposure is available). Important distinction from items 1-3: they have exposure_level = low because half-placket can't structurally close all the way; this full button-down can, so its exposure floor is genuinely none. fabric_behavior = drape (fluid, soft, follows body — textbook lyocell drape), fabric_risks = none (lyocell has good crease recovery, low wrinkle risk vs linen; the snag risk is real but doesn't map to the schema's listed risks). fabric_quality = high: 100% lyocell premium natural-derived material, real construction (tailored collar, proper cuff, back box pleat, curved hem), MD heritage — same tier as the linen polos. body_type_best = rectangle (drape adds soft volume to a straight torso), inverted_triangle (loose cut + curved hem softens broad shoulders, doesn't pull), oval (drape forgives midsection, friendlier than the linen polos' boxier fall); ok = hourglass (works tucked to create proportion, doesn't define waist on its own) and triangle (upgraded from risky vs the linen polos — drape lyocell over hips reads softer than crisp linen, override condition met). No risky tag — the soft fabric + open styling flexibility makes this universally workable. body_modulators include full_arm_friendly as a mild signal: sleeve is loose through the arm per the tech sketch, though the cuff buttons close at the wrist; not as strong as a true wide/balloon sleeve but real enough to flag. fit_failures_addressed = forgiving_midsection (loose drape + length over midsection). Style: refined_casual core + formal_polished adjacent, item_role = hero. The shirt's identity is refined_casual (drape lyocell, loose cut — MD's deliberate choice to make it less formal); formal_polished is borrowed via styling, not inherent. A true formal_polished white shirt would be crisp poplin or silk, structured cut. Worth noting at the schema level: white button-downs sit on a spectrum (crisp poplin → drape lyocell → oversized cotton) and the fabric/cut tells you the register — the type alone is too coarse. context = work, evening, formal (dropped everyday/vacation/weekend — this is a deliberate shirt, not a throw-on; tightens the use case honestly). Test-user fit: strong for Mom (refined_casual core, covers chest via full placket, drape lyocell is her register — exactly the kind of piece she wore yesterday). Sister works (refined_casual core, full_bust_friendly + full_arm_friendly support her modulators, loose drape doesn't pull). Misses Lora (not petite_friendly, and formal-leaning is outside her cool_casual closed register). Misses You partially on register (you skew more open/directional, less formal-adjacent). Net: catalog's first proper white button-down, fills the closet-staple gap, anchors refined_casual for Mom/Sister with formal_polished extension.

intent_execution_notes:
intent_5: "Worn with a relaxed or roomy top over the close mid-rise short. The fitted seat plus the short close-cut leg pulls the whole read downward — the bare leg below the hem is the focus, shown as a fitted line rather than a loose reveal. Legs are the headline. Fires for users who want room on top / focus on the lower body (Q6 = more room on top, slimmer bottom) who name legs as a featuring zone. The close fit keeps it body-engaged — leg-focus through the line, not a relaxed opening, which is why it's 5 and not 9."


intent_2: "Secondary to the leg story: the mid-rise waistband is a styling_anchor surface, so a tucked top meets it to define the waist when she wants it. styling_anchor strategy. She chooses — tuck for a defined waist, or leave the top out and let the legs lead (Intent 5). The close fit means the waist she makes reads crisp rather than bloused."
target_user_description: The rectangle, hourglass, or inverted_triangle woman, mid-short to tall, living in whimsical or whimsical-curious, who wants a bold candy-stripe statement short that reads playful and summery. Plays two strategies: styling_anchor when tucked into a tee or knit (the mid-rise structured waistband creates the proportion event, the stripe becomes the focal point), neutral when worn loose with an untucked top (the shorts read as the outfit's statement piece without the waistband doing structural work). The wildcard tag in the catalog — meant for a woman who wants a single committed-statement summer piece rather than another neutral basic. Plays Intent 9 register: body covered above + legs as the visual opening below, with the bold candy stripes providing the statement visual register (whimsical world)
why_selected: Catalog's first whimsical core piece — Stage 1 diagnostic for the whimsical world (no items currently tagged whimsical core means we can't test surfacing logic there until now). Also catalog's first genuinely red item and first bold print on a bottom (previously only the MD striped polo carried a bold pattern, and that was a top). Wildcard pick per the Part 2 brief: a piece intentionally outside the four test users' confirmed registers, designed to test the engine's discipline — does it correctly hold this back from the cool_casual closed user (Lora) and the refined_casual mom while surfacing it to whimsical/urban_ease-curious users? Adds Zara as the catalog's first true fast-fashion mid-low tier brand (vs Zara's previously catalogued knit polos and jeans, which have been higher-end Zara), which itself is a register signal: whimsical statement pieces don't need premium construction, they need the right print at the right price. Introduces pants_mom_fit as a new silhouette value (schema gap flagged in Part 9 acknowledged shorts gap, but the mom-fit cut is recurring enough to warrant the value).
known_tradeoffs: Risky for triangle — bold vertical stripes on hips/seat draw eye to her widest zone. Override fires when she accepts short hems AND celebrates legs/hips/seat as featured zones AND commits to Q6 = slimmer top, more room on bottom (signals she wears the proportion-play register where tucking a fitted top creates an upward pull that balances the stripe's downward eye-draw). Risky for oval — mid-rise at midsection + close seat + bold stripes amplify visibility at her concern zones. Override fires when she accepts short hems AND celebrates legs AND accepts close-fit bottoms. Without overrides, both filtered. 100% cotton no-stretch — mom-fit sits how it sits, no all-day stretch comfort. fabric_quality: acceptable (Zara fast-fashion mid-low tier; OCS organic certification is sourcing signal, not construction). Not petite-confirmed — held off the tag rather than guessing. Whimsical is sparsely populated; this item may sit unmatched for many users by design.
tagging_notes:
silhouette = pants_mom_fit, a new value introduced with this item to capture mom-fit's distinct geometry (relaxed through hip with soft A-line flare, structured waistband, mid-rise) — fills the schema gap flagged in Part 9 around shorts/bermudas and recurring fit names. Worth confirming at next schema audit. bottom_rise = mid per Zara's explicit "mid-waist" copy and 32cm front rise at size 34 — distinct from the Zoe's high rise. Strategy = styling_anchor + neutral: the mid-rise structured waistband (cotton denim, no stretch) is enough of a tucking platform to commit to anchor when tucked, and the cut fades to neutral when worn untucked. closeness_to_body_bottom = close: seat sits close per back model shot (patch pockets flat against the seat), then flares A-line from there. volume = none (relaxed ≠ volume per Principle 3; the A-line is natural denim cut, not exaggerated fullness). pattern = bold (vertical red-and-white stripes are a textbook bold print). color_family = red (red dominates visually against the white), color_density = colorful (saturated red, high contrast). body_visibility_demand = medium: mom-fit sits close at waist and seat without tracing, and the stripes amplify the visible area — earns medium, doesn't reach high. exposure_level = medium_high (3"-inseam micro-short — short-shorts threshold per the audit call this turn; same correction retroactively applied to item 28 the Zoe). body_type_best includes inverted_triangle (the unusual call): bold-print mom shorts on the lower body actively balance a top-heavy frame, drawing the eye down — this is one of the rare items where inverted_triangle isn't just "ok" but actively served. body_type_risky = triangle + oval, both with named overrides (celebrate-hips/legs or permissive style for triangle; celebrate-legs for oval). No modulators apply cleanly (mid-rise doesn't earn midsection_friendly, and the bold stripe contradicts curvy_lower_body_friendly's principle). fit_failures_addressed = none (no stretch in 100% cotton denim, mid-rise isn't midsection-forgiving). Scale = mid_short through tall — petite deliberately held off per discipline (no review evidence, the Madewell pattern of "uncertain → skip" applies). core_style = whimsical, single core no adjacent: bold candy stripes are the textbook playfulness signal that earns whimsical, and the print is the identity — urban_ease was tempting but felt like a softer urban_ease user wouldn't fully embrace the retro-summer register that whimsical owns. item_role = hero (committed statement piece, defines whimsical world entry — basics don't commit, this does). context = everyday, mom_duty, vacation, weekend: mom_duty included on the realistic-wear test (good shorts to put on with a white tee and leave the house) rather than the register-coherence test (statement piece reads as not-mom_duty); going with realistic-wear lens since it's closer to how the engine should match. styling_flexibility = closed (single garment, not convertible). Test-user fit: primary target is You (urban_ease/whimsical-curious, statement-tolerant, range of registers) — this is the wildcard hero the catalog has been short on. Possible secondary for Sister if she's whimsical-curious within refined_casual base. Misses Mom cleanly (refined_casual, would never wear this — exactly the discipline test: engine should correctly not surface). Misses Lora cleanly (cool_casual closed, statement piece is outside her register — second discipline test). Net: catalog's first whimsical anchor, the wildcard diagnostic for Stage 1, and the test of whether the engine respects register boundaries on a loud item.
Intents_served: intent_9 only. The shorts deliver legs as designed opening on a body-covered (loose top untucked) outfit. Intent 2 considered but doesn't fire — the bold stripes are the visual headline of the outfit, not the waist event; even when tucked, the stripe statement dominates the silhouette read, so the waist isn't the primary visual story (per the locked Intent 2 principle from item 26: Intent 2 requires waist to be the visual headline, not just mechanically possible via tuck). Intent 4 considered but doesn't fire — mom-fit A-line is too subtle for voluminous bottom + fitted top amplification mechanism

intent_execution_notes:
intent_2: "The boxy cut terminates at the natural waist, so the hem itself marks the waistline — the length does the waist work worn loose, and it tucks cleanly for a sharper line. styling_anchor strategy: she gets a defined waist from where the shirt ends, on her terms, without a tight or traced fit. Fires for the independent definer (Q5a = tuck) and especially for shorter users, where a waist-length crop lands right at the natural waist — a relaxed body that still keeps a waist. On a taller frame the same length can sit above the waist and read Intent 6 instead; the crop is the affordance."

intent_6: "Worn untucked over relaxed or fitted bottom. Boxy interlock cut + waist-length hem + no featuring zones = clean step-back register. Body engaged softly through the structured knit hang rather than traced or hidden. The compact interlock holds shape rather than draping, so the silhouette reads as deliberate-relaxed rather than slouchy. Step-back basic for users in Intent 6 mode who want body disengagement without going to oversized."
